item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This new image is really not great… There's a large, very visible photoshop outline around the astronaut where it was clearly cut out of a different picture, one that doesn't match the rest of the background. The astronaut is clearly superimposed in and it doesn't work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I seem to recall a behind the scenes post showing how the image was made, essentially using a small figurine suspended by some fishing wire in front of a black backdrop. Did the creator of the article rescind permission to use that image?

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with an image that's cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and is sourced as follows:

Title: 1959-new.png
Author(s): National Aeronautics and Space Administration, Yossipossi
Release year: 2020
Note: Created by Yossipossi as a cropped and edited version of the image below.

Source: Wikimedia Commons
License: Public Domain
Title: SuitSat after release.jpg
Author: National Aeronautics and Space Administration

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Somber. Short. I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Shouldnt this be Keter since its not contained?

Also, i believe astronauts once tested how long they could wear a space suit for. They all vomited after like 9 hours or so. This guy is probably not having a good time. I mean, on top of the fact that he is in this situation to begin with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Probably shouldn't bother on something this old and popular, but you state he's orbiting at a constant(ish) speed, but that he's also transitioning from low-Earth to high-Earth orbit. That isn't how orbital mechanics work. Speed is the fundamental determiner of where you're orbiting. As you get closer to Earth, you have to and will naturally speed up to swing further out again. The speed is the sole determiner of how far your orbit is, so you can either have a stable, circular orbit where the speed is constant, or an eccentric (oval-shaped) orbit that takes you closer and further with varying speed. Can't really have both, at least not at any given moment.

You could fix this by giving your cosmonaut random accelerations or decelerations from time to time which change his orbit, which I think would be even scarier, and would give a stronger reason for why he can't be recovered easily.

item.title: Good lord.
item.content:encoded: The idea of getting lost in space makes my skin crawl. Good job, you [EXPLETIVE REDACTED].

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good news us that, technically, you'd probably pass out almost immediately if your visor suddenly broke.

Back in the space race, IIRC, NASA was testing spacesuit designs in controlled environments. They got to people-testing, and one of the suits had a bad seal or something. Anyway, the astronaut suffered from vacuum exposure when they depressurized the testing lab, and he almost immediately falls over unconcious.

Don't worry, he got better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who is extremely astrophobic, I can say without a doubt this is the most terrifying skip to me, simply because that's the living embodiment of my worst fear.

I'd try to break my visor, too. The sudden, horrifying death of spacesuit decompression is at least quicker than the slow torment of this poor soul's situation

Someone PLEASE put that man out of his misery

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm, I didn't know that. Thanks for letting me know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a bit of a necro-reply but is worth mentioning that the photo is realistic. Stars do not normally show in photographs from orbit or the moon etc . This is because stars are too faint to be detected by most cameras. You would need to use special cameras (which I guess the foundation wouldn't bother with because the stars are not important in this case) or a long exposure which would prevent you getting an image of the actual scp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so damn disturbing. We can assume that it's a Soviet cosmonaut inside of that thing, and we can assume that there's a reason he's up there, but there's just so much that isn't said, and that is terrifying. And what isn't said very easily challenges what assumptions can be made.

What happened that his spacesuit is now invincible? What happened to break the visor? What made the visor mist over? Why is he trying to break his own visor? Is there even any certainty that what's inside of that spacesuit is human?

This is nightmare fuel. It really is. I've liked this since I first read it, and this will likely remain one of my favorites forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit more expansion would be good, but still awesome. UPVOTED.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A more obvious, but more fitting, piece, methinks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some music I believe fits this poor chap.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: part of the charm of this one is because it's short and sweet, like alot of the original series. Just a strange glitch in the universe.

item.title: Re: SCP-1959
item.content:encoded:
Thank you.

EDIT: I wanted to thank Thekillerax, but I couldn't think of anything else to say, and didn't want to just post two words, so for that reason I have made 1959 pink.

You are all welcome, for my spam prevention.

item.title: Re: SCP-1959
item.content:encoded: These look very nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of the speech that Nixon had prepared in case the astronauts were stranded at the moon. It included instructions for a clergyman to say the prayer for burial at sea, but to replace "the deep" with "the deepest of the deep." Both beautiful, and absolutely terrifying.

item.title: Re: SCP-1959
item.content:encoded:
Previous post by Thekillerax suggests adding noise to the picture to resemble stars.

Like this perhaps. Something tells me now there are too many stars or they aren't bright enough, I don't know, my brain has been pretty fried since I joined here, bad timing. If somebody wants me to keep working on it, I can do that.

EDIT: Just went ahead and started making alternate versions. I think we're good now.

I'm not sure if the image here is cleared for Creative Commons, or is cleared for modification, so for whoever it's relevant, the picture is painting "Cosmonaut 4" by Jeremy Geddes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I completely agree. It tells you just enough to communicate the horror. Any more would be "showing the monster."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this article, but I feel the picture is a bit unrealistic. Maybe just add in a few white dots or something to give it a more genuine space feel. Right now, the image just looks like an astronaut in Limbo, not space. I understand that the lack of light is there to emphasize the bleakness of space, but still. A few tiny white dots won't detract from the article, would it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the rare cases where brevity works wonders. What exactly could be added to this article to make it better? To me, its shortness really hammers in the horror of the concept. There's no extraneous testing logs or interviews that bog down the lede… just a poor, desperate man, isolated in the extreme emptiness of space, unable to be rescued and unable to be given the blessing of death, forced to float through the black void for eternity. I don't really think anything could be added that could improve this article. If it ain't broke, don't fix it, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I realy like this SCP. But I wish there was more to it. Love the idea but I hate how short it is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't believe that I missed that part, sorry about that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-1959 will ram through any obstacle it encounters, causing grave structural damage.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They can't just simply fly up into space and grab it.
It would take weeks just to get to it.
It would be difficult.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wouldn't the foundation be eager to obtain this SCP? It's simply orbiting earth which shouldn't be very difficult for the foundation to intercept and contain. Simply leaving a Euclid class SCP loose for multiple space related organizations to view and leaving the risk of it being thrown off course away from or towards earth seems like a good reason for the foundation to obtain it as fast as possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of me likes to think that the cosmonaut is Major Tom from 'Space Oddity'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was direct and straight to the point, although I wished they explained a bit on how they know about the visibility of seeing from within the helmet. I also have to admit that the real horror is being 1959, as you are blinded by your visor constantly spinning around earth and smashing into whatever's out there.

I also find it clever that the article's # designation is 1959.

item.title: Re: This movie make me remind SCP-1959
item.content:encoded: I have to say, I didn't really 'get' this skip before I saw the movie. Now I find it terrifying.
It's good. I don't really like the idea, but for some reason I see nothing wrong.

item.title: Unsettling
item.content:encoded: This is a description that more SCP articles need. It is harder to achieve and therefore all the more powerful when accomplished. congrats and +1.
Damn interesting and creepy as shit article.

item.title: This movie make me remind SCP-1959
item.content:encoded:
+ http://www.youtube.com/embed/ufsrgE0BYf0?rel=0
- http://www.youtube.com/embed/ufsrgE0BYf0?rel=0

This movie is named 'Gravity'. It is about a spacewoman who get lost by a space accident.
When I saw the last part of the trailer (1:17), I felt so sad.
She was just like SCP-1959, the lost cosmonaut in the space. How sad, isn't it?

+ http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/local--files/dr-kim/scp-1959.jpg
- http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/local--files/dr-kim/scp-1959.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one. It's not funny, creepy, or scary. It's more…. Unsettling. When you read this and look at the picture and understand the point, something squirms inside of you.

The fact that it gives this atmosphere is what's good about this.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably because of the radiation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I don't understand why the Foundation doesn't just commandeer a shuttle to go up and get the thing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn that is….that is ominous. And the picture just nails it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wow. I hate all the time on SCPs that don't do enough with their concept, but this one is one of the few cases where a short article that doesn't flesh out much nails the right tone for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know it's been over a year since this comment was posted, but nobody else replied and I wanted to thank you for making the reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone needs to write a tale from the perspective of this guy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very creepy. I remember reading about the "lost" Soviet cosmonauts. The Torre Bert Recordings were particularly chilling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent rewrite. Lonely and creepy without resorting to gross-out tactics or cheap scares.
Congrats on the +100! This article is good and you should feel good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This one always makes me sad. Just imagining the loneliness makes me uneasy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This remains one of the creepiest SCPs I've ever read that didn't have a nightmare fuel image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nice rewrite. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I…how did I miss this until today? This is great. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is very good, and it's also impressive reading all these comments and realizing just how much work was put into the rewrite. Nicely done!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one a lot but whatever course he's on, it's certainly not an orbit!

Radioactive, in a spacesuit he can't see out of for DECADES… And you though your job sucked!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well. Here I am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely terrifying. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of The Man Who Walked Home by James Tiptree, Jr.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a man who believes in the Lost Cosmonauts theory, this SCP is a perfect and horrorific tribute to what could have actually happenned. Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't great just because I'm a sucker for space-themed things, but also because it's genuinely terrifying. Great work!

Although it may be better for us all to have some restraint, I must admit it would be fun to take opportunity of the potential "number play" we now have and see some more period-themed SCPs.

Oh, and I must say the picture really adds to this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loving the minimalism. Upote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but I worry that it's too similar to something I've seen elsewhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Much better!
